keepers_cottage.enter: A small stone cottage. The fire went out. The last Keeper's journal lies open on the table.
keepers_cottage.examine_journal: The final entry reads: 'The mechanism won't hold. Three nights, three failures. It knows when I'm close.'
keepers_cottage.examine_fire: Cold ash. At least three days since it was lit.
keepers_cottage.desc: A modest dwelling near the cliff path. The smell of old paper and sea salt. Maren tends the archive here.

lighthouse_base.enter: The lighthouse rises above you. The door is unlocked, but something about the threshold resists you.
lighthouse_base.examine_door: Salt-crusted iron hinges. Fresh scratch marks around the lock.
lighthouse_base.desc: The base of the great tower. Its shadow falls long across the rocks. The mechanism hums faintly.

lighthouse_top.enter: The lens burns with a cold light. From up here, the whole island looks like a diagram of something.
lighthouse_top.desc: The lens room. From here you can see the whole island — and something in the water below.

harbor.enter: The harbour is quiet. Too quiet for a working port. A single lantern burns at the harbormaster's office.
harbor.examine_boats: The boats are tied down. Supplies are still aboard.
harbor.desc: Weathered boats creak against the dock. Silas watches the horizon. The fog is thick this morning.

village_square.enter: The village square. A few residents hurry past without making eye contact.
village_square.examine_well: The well has been sealed with planks. Nailed shut from above.
village_square.desc: The heart of the island. Villagers move with purpose, eyes cast low. The well stands dry.

ruins.enter: Older than the village. Older, perhaps, than the island itself. The stones don't fit together right.
ruins.examine_stones: Maritime runes. But they predate any known maritime civilisation.
ruins.desc: Crumbled walls of what was once a manor. The east wing still stands, barely. The smell of old smoke that will not quite leave.

mechanism_room.enter: Gears the size of millstones. Whatever drove them once, it wasn't wind or water.
mechanism_room.desc: Gears the size of millstones turn without apparent power. The hum from above is louder here. The walls are covered in symbols no one on the island claims to recognise.

chapel.enter: St. Maret's Chapel. The doors are never locked. Someone has been lighting candles.
chapel.desc: The pews are empty but the candles are lit. Someone tends this place. The stained glass is cracked; the saint's face replaced with rough plaster.

mill.enter: The old mill creaks in a wind that isn't there. The wheel hasn't turned in years.
mill.desc: The wheel still turns though the stream has been dry for years. The miller's log is open on the table, the last entry three weeks ago.

forest_path.enter: The treeline begins here. Strange how the path seems different every time you walk it.
forest_path.desc: The trees press close. Roots cross the path like sleeping fingers. You hear movement that stops when you stop.

cliffside.enter: The cliff edge drops sharply to the cove below. At certain hours, something moves in the water.
cliffside.desc: Wind tears at your coat. The cliff drops eighty feet to the rocks below. You can see the lighthouse from here — and the shape beneath the water.

archive_basement.enter: Row upon row of stone shelves. Whatever is catalogued here predates the village by centuries.
archive_basement.desc: Stone steps descending into cold air. Rows of iron shelving. Maren's domain. Water stains mark the walls at hip height — the flood came this far, once.

tidal_caves.enter: The tide retreats to reveal caves no one has mapped. The walls are covered in writing.
tidal_caves.desc: Seawater pools on the cave floor, lit from below by something bioluminescent. The tide is coming in. You have perhaps an hour.

vision.loop5: You see the lighthouse as it was. Full of light. Full of people. They were not afraid then.
vision.loop10: A Keeper — not you — walks toward the mechanism room. She does not come back out.
vision.loop15: The thing beneath the island has a face. You have seen it before. In your reflection.

vael_whisper.title: ◈  VISION: THE LIGHTHOUSE'S PURPOSE  ◈
vael_whisper.text: The lighthouse does not guide ships. It keeps something contained. The beam does not reach outward — it holds inward. Every night you turn the light on, you are not saving sailors. You are renewing a lock. You have been turning the key without knowing what lies behind the door.

keeper_memory.title: ◈  VISION: THE KEEPER'S BARGAIN  ◈
keeper_memory.text: A memory not your own: the previous Keeper standing at the mechanism room at midnight. Something old and patient waited in the dark below, its patience measured in centuries. The Keeper spoke words they did not fully understand, in a language older than the island. The mechanism accepted the bargain. What the Keeper paid came due much later, and all at once.

island_truth.title: ◈  VISION: THE DRIFTING ISLE  ◈
island_truth.text: The island has moved. It appears on no maps because it chooses who finds it. Ships do not navigate to it — the island navigates to them, drifting unseen between one chart and the next. You were not lucky to wash ashore. You were selected. The question left unasked is: selected by whom, and for what purpose.

threshold_call.title: ◈  VISION: THE DOOR BELOW  ◈
threshold_call.text: The mechanism beneath the lighthouse is a door. It has been waiting. Not for the right key or the correct keeper — it has been waiting for someone willing. The question was never whether the door opens. The door opens. The question is what you choose to do when it does, and what comes through in the moment of your choosing.

lighthouse_ignition.title: Ignition
lighthouse_ignition.text: The mechanism responds. Ancient gears turn for the first time in decades. The light fractures through crystal lenses — not white, but amber, like trapped sunlight from another age.

keeper_echo.title: The Echo
keeper_echo.text: A figure stands beside you — translucent, wearing your face but older. "You understand now," it says. "Every keeper who came before left something behind. So will you."

final_resonance.title: Resonance
final_resonance.text: The beam sweeps the water. Something vast stirs beneath. You feel the island shudder, grateful and afraid.

history_01.title: The First Keeper
history_01.body: Fragmentary records of the lighthouse's first appointed keeper, whose name has been struck from the official register. What remains suggests someone who arrived already knowing things they should not have known. The register does not say who made the deletion, or when.

history_02.title: Founding Charter
history_02.body: A partial transcription of the island's founding charter. Several clauses have been deliberately obscured — not damaged, but carefully redacted with a practised hand. The surviving language binds future keepers to obligations that are never fully described.

history_03.title: The Great Storm
history_03.body: Accounts of the storm of 1743 diverge significantly. Some speak of a light that never went out despite the mechanism being reported destroyed. Three separate journals record the same dream on the same night: the lighthouse walking into the sea.

history_04.title: The Survey
history_04.body: An old surveyor's report noting geological anomalies beneath the ruins that do not appear in the final submitted version. The original draft was found inside a sealed wall cavity during unrelated repairs. Someone had read it — the margins are annotated.

history_05.title: Keeper's Log: Year One
history_05.body: The first keeper wrote with the cautious pride of someone new to responsibility. By year's end the handwriting had changed — not the hand of a different person, but the same hand aged by something other than time. The final entry is dated three months before the official departure.

history_06.title: The Rotation
history_06.body: Lighthouse keepers were meant to rotate every three years. Records show some stayed considerably longer — one for eleven years, another for what the log ambiguously records as 'continuing service.' The mainland authority has no record of extending any of their contracts.

history_07.title: Last Official Correspondence
history_07.body: The final letter to the mainland authority describes routine operations and requests a supply shipment. It was never answered. The supply shipment arrived six weeks later, was offloaded by persons unknown, and the ship's log records no contact with any resident.

history_08.title: The Parish Record
history_08.body: Births and deaths recorded by the chapel. The death entries stop abruptly in a particular year — not because deaths ceased, but because whoever was recording them did. The final entry reads: 'I no longer know what to call this.'

history_09.title: The Annexe
history_09.body: A small room behind the chapel altar is not on any official plan of the building. It is on every unofficial one. The door has no lock but has never been found open by anyone who did not arrive intending to open it.

history_10.title: Oral Histories
history_10.body: Three contradictory accounts of what happened when the last Keeper disappeared. All three are probably true — the events they describe are not mutually exclusive, only impossible to have occurred simultaneously under normal conditions. The island does not always operate under normal conditions.

occult_01.title: The Binding Marks
occult_01.body: The symbols carved into the ruins predate any known written language. They share structural features with musical notation — intervals, rests, and something that may indicate duration. Played at the right frequency, a recording of them causes equipment interference.

occult_02.title: Resonance Theory
occult_02.body: Whoever carved these marks understood something about sound, light, and attention that we do not. The marks are not decorative and not linguistic — they are instructional. The instructions are for something that has not yet been attempted, or has been attempted and not survived.

occult_03.title: The Loop Index
occult_03.body: A set of tally marks at the ruins that no one has been able to explain. The count is very high — higher than any plausible human lifespan would permit. Beside the tallies, in newer marks, someone has written: 'still not finished.'

occult_04.title: The Old Name
occult_04.body: The entity beneath has a name in the old script. It roughly translates as 'the one that remembers the drowned.' This is considered a description rather than a proper name — the entity's actual designation, if it has one, appears only in a text that has not been found.

occult_05.title: The Cliff Vigil
occult_05.body: Someone has been keeping watch from the cliffside for a very long time. The worn stone suggests decades of the same posture, the same position. Whatever they were watching for either arrived or they gave up — the depression in the rock stops being renewed at some point.

occult_06.title: The Feeding Pattern
occult_06.body: The entity does not sleep. It simply waits for the beam to align — a specific angle that occurs twice in the lighthouse's rotation, lasting approximately four seconds each time. During those four seconds, instruments in the archive show readings they have no scale for.

occult_07.title: Echo Residue
occult_07.body: Echoes of prior keepers concentrate near the cliff edge. The resonance is measurable if you know what to measure. Three separate investigators have recorded the same whispered phrase — each thought it was their own thought until they compared notes.

occult_08.title: The Tide Script
occult_08.body: The writing on the cave walls is rewritten by the tides. Something underneath keeps restoring it — the same words, the same placement, with minor variations that suggest refinement rather than damage. The text has been improving for as long as anyone has been watching.

occult_09.title: The Deep Chamber
occult_09.body: Behind the main cave there is a chamber that only opens under specific tidal conditions. What is inside has been documented three times. All three accounts agree on the geometry but disagree entirely on the contents.

occult_10.title: The Final Binding
occult_10.body: The original binding required three keepers. The seal has never been properly completed — only two ever agreed simultaneously. The third position has been vacant since the third year of the lighthouse's operation, waiting for someone who understands what they are agreeing to.

maritime_01.title: Shipping Manifests
maritime_01.body: The last recorded arrival was seventeen months ago. The manifest lists cargo that was never offloaded — it remains, presumably, aboard a ship that has also not been accounted for. The harbour shows no signs of unusual occupation.

maritime_02.title: The Harbour Depth
maritime_02.body: The harbour was dredged three times in the last century. Each time they found something and stopped. The dredging reports are filed under administrative delay pending further review — a status that has persisted for forty years.

maritime_03.title: Navigation Anomalies
maritime_03.body: Compasses behave strangely within half a league of the island. The lighthouse beam does not correct for this — it amplifies it at certain angles, creating false bearings that lead ships toward the tidal caves rather than the open harbour.

maritime_04.title: The Wrecks
maritime_04.body: Twelve wrecks are on record. Local lore suggests there are forty more that were never reported — ships that arrived, were quietly taken apart, and whose crews were absorbed into the village population without formal registration. Silas knows more than he records.

maritime_05.title: The Harbormaster's Book
maritime_05.body: Silas maintains meticulous records with one exception. The three-month gap is filled with a single word, repeated across twenty-two pages. The word is in the old script. When translated, it means: witness.

maritime_06.title: Cave Tidal Charts
maritime_06.body: Handmade charts of tidal patterns in the caves, more precise than anything the Navy has produced. They predict patterns four cycles in advance with complete accuracy. They were made by someone who understood the caves from the inside.

maritime_07.title: The Swimmer's Path
maritime_07.body: A series of markers leads from the cave entrance toward deeper water. They were not made by human hands — the material is unknown and the placement required access to depths no diver has returned from. The markers are evenly spaced. They are still being added to.

maritime_08.title: Bioluminescence Log
maritime_08.body: Notes on unusual light emissions from the cave water. The pattern matches the lighthouse beam exactly — not approximately, but with a precision that would require real-time communication between the beam and whatever is producing the underwater light.

maritime_09.title: The Current
maritime_09.body: There is a persistent current that runs from the tidal caves toward open water. It flows uphill in places, which is not possible. Hydrological surveys have confirmed the measurements and declined to offer an explanation.

maritime_10.title: The Missing Boats
maritime_10.body: Three fishing boats are known to have entered the caves voluntarily. None returned. Their owners left documentation suggesting they knew this was likely. Each left the same destination in their personal records: 'the place beneath the light.'

ecology_01.title: The Forest Edge
ecology_01.body: The treeline has moved. Old maps show the forest beginning half a mile further from the lighthouse — it has advanced steadily over the last century at a rate inconsistent with natural growth. The trees at the new edge are not saplings.

ecology_02.title: Fungal Network
ecology_02.body: The root network beneath the forest path is unlike anything documented. It pulses — a slow rhythm that, when measured, matches the rotation period of the lighthouse beam. The botanist who first noted this retired from field work immediately after.

ecology_03.title: The Boundary Plants
ecology_03.body: Certain plants grow only at the exact perimeter of the lighthouse beam's reach. Nowhere else — not inside the beam's range, not outside it. They appear to mark the boundary with the precision of a surveying instrument.

ecology_04.title: Animal Behaviour
ecology_04.body: No birds nest within the beam's range. Those that fly through it do not fly straight afterward — they curve, as though the beam's passage left a physical impression in the air that persists. The curves are always in the same direction.

ecology_05.title: The Path Changes
ecology_05.body: The forest path reroutes itself between visits. Locals accept this as normal and adapt their routes accordingly. Those who insist on following their original path find it does not lead where it once did, or where they intended to go.

ecology_06.title: Cliff Flora
ecology_06.body: The plants growing from the cliff face have no known species classification. They flower at night, exclusively, and the flowers emit a faint luminescence. Petra has been cultivating specimens in controlled conditions — the results are sealed.

ecology_07.title: The Lichen
ecology_07.body: A specific lichen pattern covers the south face of the cliff. Under magnification it resolves into text — the same text that appears on the cave walls, the ruin inscriptions, and, reportedly, in the sleep imagery of anyone who spends more than a week on the island.

ecology_08.title: Sea Life Concentration
ecology_08.body: Marine life clusters beneath the cliff in numbers that shouldn't be sustainable. They are not feeding — their stomach contents show nothing. They are gathered, oriented, facing upward, toward the lighthouse. They have been there for as long as the fishermen can remember.

ecology_09.title: The Mill Mold
ecology_09.body: A species of mold is consuming the mill's timbers. It has the structural properties of bone — rigid, load-bearing, and capable of regeneration when damaged. Tobias refuses to address it. He says it is keeping the building standing.

ecology_10.title: Grain Mutations
ecology_10.body: The grain stored in the mill has been growing. In the dark, without soil, without water. The new growth is not grain — it is something else that has adopted grain's structure as a template. Tobias grinds it regardless. It makes adequate flour.

alchemy_01.title: The Preparation
alchemy_01.body: A formula for a compound that, when burned, produces a light visible only to those who have died and returned. The formula requires three standard ingredients and one that is listed only as 'what the mechanism does not consume.' The instruction notes that this ingredient is abundant on the island.

alchemy_02.title: Memory Distillation
alchemy_02.body: Notes on extracting the residue of repeated experiences — actions performed in multiple loops leave a physical trace that can be concentrated. The process requires a loop to be in progress during extraction. The product degrades rapidly but can be fixed with certain cliff flora.

alchemy_03.title: The Anchor Compound
alchemy_03.body: A substance described as capable of anchoring an echo to a specific location permanently. Ingredients: plant matter, tidal water, and one that is listed only as 'a person who agrees.' The archive holds three sealed vials of completed compound.

alchemy_04.title: Resonance Amplifiers
alchemy_04.body: Small objects that increase the sympathetic resonance between two beings — allowing partial sharing of memory, perception, and loop-experience. The Archivist has several. She does not acknowledge having them. Their manufacture is documented in Petra's sealed notes.

alchemy_05.title: Chapel Herbs
alchemy_05.body: Several unusual herbs grow in the chapel garden that are not native to this climate and were planted deliberately. Their pharmaceutical properties remain active indefinitely after drying. The chapel records describe their ritual use in terms that could equally describe chemistry.

alchemy_06.title: The Candle Formula
alchemy_06.body: The candles in the chapel are not ordinary wax. When burned in an enclosed space, the smoke carries encoded information accessible to any present who have experienced loop-memory. The formula is in plain sight in the vestry, labelled as a standard supply order.

alchemy_07.title: Sacramental Chemistry
alchemy_07.body: The original chapel rites were pharmaceutical as well as spiritual — the two were not distinguished in the founding community's practice. What the congregation understood as spiritual experience was in part chemically induced, and what they understood as chemistry was in part something else.

alchemy_08.title: The Suppressor
alchemy_08.body: A compound that prevents loop-memory from surfacing in those who have experienced multiple loops. Someone has been adding it to the village water supply for at least forty years. The concentration is precisely calibrated — enough to suppress memory, not enough to cause any other effect.

alchemy_09.title: Antidote Notes
alchemy_09.body: Incomplete notes on reversing the suppressor compound. The last page is missing. What remains suggests the reversal is straightforward but requires administration during a specific phase of the loop — the narrow window between the mechanism's last firing and the reset.

alchemy_10.title: The Final Reagent
alchemy_10.body: The one ingredient that cannot be synthesised. It must be freely given — the act of giving under duress or deception renders it inert. The notes describe every other component in precise quantities. This one is listed simply as: 'enough.'

cartography_01.title: The True Map
cartography_01.body: A map of the island that shows locations which do not appear to exist in physical space as currently experienced. Several are marked as dangerous, two as inaccessible without specific preparation. One location is marked only with the notation: 'do not name this.'

cartography_02.title: Sightline Analysis
cartography_02.body: The lighthouse was positioned to create specific sightlines to every significant location on the island. This was not incidental — the structural records show multiple rebuilds to achieve precise angles. Each sightline, when followed from the lighthouse, terminates at something that was there before the lighthouse was built.

cartography_03.title: The Beam's Reach
cartography_03.body: A precise calculation of the lighthouse beam's maximum reach under various atmospheric conditions. Under certain fog densities the beam extends further than physics should permit. The calculations note this without comment, as though the anomaly were expected.

cartography_04.title: Underground Map
cartography_04.body: A partial map of what lies beneath the island. The lower levels are marked with a symbol meaning do not continue — but the map continues anyway, in a different hand, in a different medium, as though someone found the original and felt compelled to complete it despite the warning.

cartography_05.title: The Cliff Survey
cartography_05.body: A survey of the cliff face noting entry points. Several are accessible only at specific phases of the tide. One entry point is marked with the notation: 'accessible only when the lighthouse is not in rotation' — a condition that has never been officially recorded as occurring.

cartography_06.title: The Offshore Markers
cartography_06.body: Submerged markers at specific distances from the cliff. Their purpose is not navigational — ships following them would be led away from safety rather than toward it. The distances match the intervals between the old script characters on the cave walls.

cartography_07.title: The Dead Zone
cartography_07.body: A region of sea between the cliff and the nearest shipping lane where all instruments fail completely. Compasses, chronometers, echo-sounders — all read zero, or read values that have no physical interpretation. Ships that pass through it report no awareness of the passage.

cartography_08.title: Village Original Plan
cartography_08.body: The original plan of the village differs from what was built. The deviations are not random — each building was shifted to a position that maintains a specific angular relationship to the lighthouse. The village is a diagram. The lighthouse is the subject.

cartography_09.title: The Routes
cartography_09.body: A map of paths through the village and island with markings showing which routes are safe at which times of day and phase of the loop. The map appears to have been made by someone who tested every route multiple times, in multiple loops, and recorded the variance.

cartography_10.title: Overlay Analysis
cartography_10.body: When all known maps are overlaid, a pattern emerges in the white space between them — the uncharted area forms a shape that corresponds to no feature on the island as currently mapped. The shape moves slightly between versions. Something is being tracked.

linguistics_01.title: Pre-Settlement Script
linguistics_01.body: A script predating the settlement of the island by at least four centuries. It uses the lighthouse as a reference point despite the lighthouse not existing at the time the script was created. This is either evidence of extreme antiquity in the lighthouse's origins, or the script is not as old as it appears.

linguistics_02.title: The Archive Catalogue
linguistics_02.body: A catalogue of what the archive once contained. Most items are listed as removed or destroyed. The destruction dates cluster around three specific years, widely separated. Each cluster corresponds to a period when the lighthouse's official keeper roster shows a gap.

linguistics_03.title: Translation Notes
linguistics_03.body: Maren's personal translation notes for the old script documents. The margin comments are more revealing than the translations — they record her reactions, her growing understanding, and, in the later pages, a deliberate decision to stop recording what she has understood.

linguistics_04.title: The Cipher
linguistics_04.body: A cipher used in the more sensitive archive documents. The key is a number that changes each loop — specifically, the number of times the lighthouse has completed a full rotation since the loop began. The cipher self-updates. The messages in it change accordingly.

linguistics_05.title: Comparative Glossary
linguistics_05.body: A glossary comparing the old script to modern terms. The word for keeper and the word for sacrifice are the same. So are the words for light and debt. The word for loop has no modern equivalent — the closest translation offered is: 'the breath held between question and answer.'

linguistics_06.title: Ruin Inscription A
linguistics_06.body: The longest continuous inscription at the ruins. It appears to be instructions, written in the imperative, for a process that takes multiple iterations to complete. The instructions assume the reader has already failed at least once and adjust their language accordingly.

linguistics_07.title: Ruin Inscription B
linguistics_07.body: A shorter inscription, written in a different hand, that is a direct response to Inscription A. It says no — but specifically, it says no to one clause while accepting all others. The disputed clause, when translated, concerns what happens to the keeper after the process completes.

linguistics_08.title: The Name Tablets
linguistics_08.body: Stone tablets listing names. Some names appear on multiple tablets, in different hands, separated by what appear to be centuries — not as records of different people with the same name, but as continuations of a single record. One name appears on every tablet. It is not legible.

linguistics_09.title: Chapel Marginalia
linguistics_09.body: Tiny writing in the margins of the chapel's record books, written by someone with access to the old script and awareness of its meaning. It offers corrections to the official record — not to the facts, but to what the facts mean. The corrections are consistently darker in tone than the original text.

linguistics_10.title: The Repeating Phrase
linguistics_10.body: A phrase that appears in every document in the archive, in every language and script, without exception. It means approximately: it is not finished. The phrase appears in places where it could not have been added after the document's original creation — embedded in the paper itself in some cases, legible only under specific light.

old_journal.label: Old Journal
old_journal.text: The final entry reads: 'The mechanism won't hold. Three nights, three failures. It knows when I'm close.'

mechanism_diagram.label: Mechanism Diagram
mechanism_diagram.text: A hand-drawn diagram of interlocking gears and rune-marked stones. Someone has circled one node in red ink and written: 'THIS ONE FIRST.'

lens_inscription.label: Lens Inscription
lens_inscription.text: Etched around the base of the lens housing: 'Light does not travel. Light remembers.' Below that, in a different hand: 'She was right.'

keepers_last_log.label: Keeper's Last Log
keepers_last_log.text: The last entry is dated three loops ago by your count. It ends mid-sentence: 'The light went out and something came through the —'

sealed_ledger.label: Sealed Ledger
sealed_ledger.text: The ledger is sealed with wax bearing a mark you don't recognise. When you break it, the pages inside are filled with names — hundreds of names — and beside each one, a date and the word: LOOP.

water_line_mark.label: Water Line Mark
water_line_mark.text: The stain on the wall reaches to your hip. The flood came from below, not above. Something in the lower chambers holds water — or held it back.

manifest_discrepancy.label: Manifest Discrepancy
manifest_discrepancy.text: The supply manifest lists seventeen crates of 'astronomical instruments.' The dock record shows only four arrived. The rest are logged as 'retained by order of the Keeper.'

silas_logbook.label: Silas's Logbook
silas_logbook.text: Silas has been recording the tides for years. They don't match the charts. The pattern matches something else — a rhythm you've heard before, from the mechanism room.

mechanism_symbols.label: Mechanism Symbols
mechanism_symbols.text: The symbols cover every surface. You recognise three of them from the ruins. Three more from the chapel. One you have only seen in your own reflection, in the moment before the loop resets.

binding_rune.label: Binding Rune
binding_rune.text: This rune is different from the others — newer, cut with a different tool. Someone added it. Someone who came after the original builders. Someone who was trying to stop something, or seal it in.

burned_portrait.label: Burned Portrait
burned_portrait.text: The portrait is burned at the edges, the face obscured. The posture, the clothing — it could be anyone. But the ring on the painted hand matches the one Maren wears.

east_wing_door.label: East Wing Door
east_wing_door.text: The door is sealed from the inside. You can feel warmth through the wood — the warmth of a fire, or something older. There is no keyhole. The door opens from the inside, or not at all.

hidden_names.label: Hidden Names
hidden_names.text: Behind the altar cloth: names scratched into the stone. Some you recognise — NPC names, your own name spelled three different ways, and one name you have never heard but which feels, somehow, like your own.

cracked_saint.label: Cracked Saint
cracked_saint.text: The saint's face has been replaced with rough plaster. Beneath the plaster, something different — not a saint, but a figure with its hands raised not in blessing but in warning.

shape_in_water.label: Shape in Water
shape_in_water.text: At certain angles the shape beneath the water becomes clear: it is a door. A vast door set into the rock of the island's foundation. It is open. It has always been open.

rope_marks_on_rock.label: Rope Marks on Rock
rope_marks_on_rock.text: The grooves in the rock are worn deep — decades of use, at least. Someone has been lowering things into the cove, or raising them. The most recent marks are fresh.

bioluminescent_symbols.label: Bioluminescent Symbols
bioluminescent_symbols.text: The symbols glow in the same pattern as the mechanism room's walls. But here they are alive — pulsing, shifting. They are not carved. They grew here.

waterlogged_chest.label: Waterlogged Chest
waterlogged_chest.text: Inside the chest: three lighthouse keeper's logs, each from a different loop, each describing the same final day from a different perspective. All three end with the same word: 'Again.'

disturbed_earth.label: Disturbed Earth
disturbed_earth.text: Something has been buried here recently. The soil is dark and fresh. You don't dig it up. You're not sure you want to know what's there — but you mark it in your mind.

carved_tree.label: Carved Tree
carved_tree.text: The carving reads: 'I WAS HERE BEFORE YOU. I WILL BE HERE AFTER. DO NOT LIGHT THE LIGHT.' Beneath it, in smaller letters: 'Ignore this. Light it anyway. — K'

dry_well.label: Dry Well
dry_well.text: The well is sealed with planks nailed from above. You pry one loose and shine your light down. The well doesn't have a bottom you can see — but you can hear, from very far below, the sound of water, and something else moving through it.

notice_board.label: Notice Board
notice_board.text: Most notices are mundane — tides, market days. One is different: 'MISSING: The third loop. If found, do not return it. Burn it.' No signature. The notice looks new.

millers_last_log.label: Miller's Last Log
millers_last_log.text: The log ends three weeks ago with: 'The wheel turns without water. I have stopped trying to explain it. I have started writing down what it says.' The following pages are filled with symbols.

hidden_trapdoor.label: Hidden Trapdoor
hidden_trapdoor.text: The trapdoor leads down to a small chamber containing nothing except a chair, a burned-out candle, and scratched into the floor: the same loop-count you are currently on.

vael_origin.name: The Vael's True Nature
vael_origin.title: The Vael's True Nature
vael_origin.desc: Not a monster. An ancient intelligence, wrongly imprisoned within the mechanism a century ago by keepers who feared what they did not understand.

mechanism_purpose.name: The Lighthouse Mechanism
mechanism_purpose.title: The Mechanism's Purpose
mechanism_purpose.desc: The lighthouse does not merely guide ships. The mechanism beneath it is a binding engine — it exists to trap, not illuminate.

light_source_truth.name: The Light's Source
light_source_truth.title: The Light's Source
light_source_truth.desc: The lighthouse burns on no oil, no gas. It feeds on something else entirely. The lens inscription names it: 'borrowed life.'

keeper_betrayal.name: The Keeper's Pact
keeper_betrayal.title: The Keeper's Fate
keeper_betrayal.desc: The previous Keeper discovered the truth and tried to leave. They never reached the mainland. The last log entry is dated three weeks before anyone reported them missing.

spirit_binding.name: Bonds of the Old Covenant
spirit_binding.title: The Binding Ritual
spirit_binding.desc: The runes in the mechanism room are not decorative. They are a cage. Whoever conducted the original binding knew exactly what they were doing — and chose to do it anyway.

island_history.name: The Drifting Isle
island_history.title: What This Island Was
island_history.desc: Before the lighthouse, before the village, there was a place of convergence. The Vael was not summoned here — it was born here. The island was built around it.

final_signal.name: The Final Transmission
final_signal.title: The Final Transmission
final_signal.desc: On the night the mechanism fails completely, the lighthouse sends one last signal. Not to ships. To something much further away. You have seen it. You cannot unsee it.

light_covenant.name: The Light's Promise
light_covenant.title: The Light's Promise
light_covenant.desc: The lighthouse's light is not a beacon — it is a covenant renewed each night. Break the covenant once and the island remembers. Break it twice and something older than memory takes notice.

warden_truth.name: The Warden's Secret
warden_truth.title: The Warden's Secret
warden_truth.desc: There is a Warden beneath the island. Not the Vael — something that predates it. The Vael is not the prisoner. The Vael is the guard. And the guard has grown tired.

light_source_truth.title: The Light's True Source
light_source_truth.description: Something feeds the lighthouse that has nothing to do with oil or electricity.

step.talk_to_vael: Speak with the Echo about what you've seen.
step.examine_tidal_caves: Examine the bioluminescent light in the tidal caves.

archivist_bargain.title: The Archivist's Price
archivist_bargain.description: Maren knows more than she shares. The price of her knowledge is unclear.

step.talk_maren_tier2: Return to Maren once you've seen enough.

harbor_silence.title: What the Harbor Forgot
harbor_silence.description: Silas has kept a secret for twelve years. Something happened at the harbour the night the last Keeper vanished.

step.talk_silas: Talk to Silas about the night in question.

vael_origin.title: Echoes in the Deep
vael_origin.description: Something ancient stirs beneath the island. Vael knows what it is — but will not say freely.

step.examine_symbols: Examine the bioluminescent symbols in the tidal caves.
step.examine_binding: Study the binding rune in the mechanism room.
step.talk_vael_again: Return to Vael and ask about what you've found.

keeper_betrayal.title: The Last Entry
keeper_betrayal.description: The previous Keeper's final log holds a secret the island has been protecting for decades.

step.talk_maren: Ask Maren what she knows about the previous Keeper.
step.examine_ledger: Find the sealed ledger in the archive basement.

spirit_binding.title: Chains Beneath the Stone
spirit_binding.description: The mechanism's symbols are not decorative. They are a record of a crime.

step.examine_binding_rune: Examine the binding rune in the mechanism room.
step.examine_hidden_names: Find the hidden names in the chapel.
step.talk_elara: Talk to Elara about what the names mean.

island_history.title: What the Archives Forgot
island_history.description: The archive's gaps are not accidental. Someone decided what the island would remember.

step.examine_portrait: Examine the burned portrait in the ruins.
step.examine_hidden_names: Find the hidden names in the chapel.
step.talk_maren: Return to Maren with what you've found.

final_signal.title: The Last Frequency
final_signal.description: You have gathered what there is to know. The lighthouse has been waiting for this moment since before you arrived.

step.visit_top_night: Ascend to the lighthouse top while it burns.
step.talk_vael_tier3: Find Vael and speak with them one final time.
